segments.purgatorio-12-g1: Di pari is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the same way / likewise.” Di is the preposition used idiomatically here ('of' in the lemma list) and pari is an adjective used adverbially ('equal').
segments.purgatorio-12-g2: Come introduces a simile meaning “like / as.” Buoi is the noun (oxen) but the provided senses list did not include that meaning, so it is handled below as unmatched; che is the relative pronoun “who/which”; vanno is the present indicative “they go” (here used generically); a giogo is a prepositional phrase “to the yoke,” literally “yoked.”
segments.purgatorio-12-g3: M’andava io: the clitic m’ (mi) + verb andava (imperfect of andare) with the subject pronoun io placed after the verb for emphasis — literally “to me went I,” i.e. “I was going.” Con + quell’ anima carca is a prepositional phrase “with that burdened soul”; quell’ is the elided form of quello before a vowel.
segments.purgatorio-12-g4: Fin che is a temporal conjunction meaning “until.” ’l is the elided pronoun/article (here part of the clausal subject), sofferse is the perfect/remote past singular of soffrire used here in the sense “permitted / allowed / suffered” (an archaic sense: ‘to suffer’ = ‘to allow’), and il dolce pedagogo is the noun phrase “the sweet/gentle pedagogue.” The clause means “until the gentle pedagogue let it happen.”
segments.purgatorio-12-g5: Ma quando disse: is straightforward: Ma = “but,” quando = temporal conjunction “when,” and disse = past definite of dire “said.” It introduces reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-12-g6: Lascia is an imperative (“leave, abandon, let be”); lui = “him”; e = “and”; varca is an imperative of varcare (“cross” or “pass over”). The clause is direct speech with two commands.
segments.purgatorio-12-g7: Ché (short for perché) introduces an explanatory clause “for / because”; qui = “here”; è = present of essere “is”; buono = “good / suitable” (here: ‘it is appropriate/effective’); con l’ali e coi remi = “with wings and with oars” (l’ali = the wings; coi = con + i).
segments.purgatorio-12-g8: Quantunque può is concessive: quantunque = “however much / insofar as,” and può = present of potere “can / is able.” The phrase limits the following statement.
segments.purgatorio-12-g9: Ciascun = “each / everyone”; pinger in the printed lemma list gives senses related to painting/depicting, but here it functions in the sense “to propel / push” (an idiomatic or dialectal use) which is not present in the provided sense list, so marked unmatched; sua = “his/one’s own”; barca = “boat.” The clause means “let each row/propel his own boat.”
segments.purgatorio-12-g10: Dritto sì come andar vuolsi rife’mi con la persona is compact: dritto = “upright / straight,” sì = “so / thus,” come = “as,” andar vuolsi = impersonal construction roughly “one ought to go” or “one must proceed,” rife’mi is an irregular/contracted form (not matched by the provided senses; it functions here as a verb meaning roughly “I made myself ready / I resumed myself”) and mi is the clitic; con la persona = “with the person / with my body” (persona here = body/physical self). The whole means he straightened/resumed his body to go.
segments.purgatorio-12-g11: Avvegna che introduces a concessive clause “although / even though.” I pensieri = “the thoughts”; mi rimanessero is imperfect subjunctive (rimanessero) with mi clitic, “remained to me” i.e. “remained with me”; e chinati e scemi are two adjectives describing those thoughts: chinati = “bowed / bent”; scemi here means “dull / stupefied / emptied,” an older sense (the sense list did not give a direct adjectival match for scemi, see note).
segments.purgatorio-12-g12: Io m’era mosso uses the pluperfect-like periphrasis (era + past participle) to indicate movement already begun: Io = “I,” m’ = mi (reflexive/clitic), era = auxiliary “was,” mosso = past participle “moved.” The construction = “I had moved / had set out.”
segments.purgatorio-12-g13: E seguia volontieri del mio maestro i passi: seguia (imperfect of seguire) “I followed / was following,” volontieri = “gladly,” del mio maestro = “of my master,” i passi = “the steps / footsteps.” Word order places the object phrase after the adverb for emphasis but is otherwise standard.
segments.purgatorio-12-g14: E amendue già mostravam com’ eravam leggeri: e = “and,” amendue = “both” (both of us), già = “already,” mostravam (imperfect of mostrare) = “were showing / displayed,” com’ = contracted come “how/as,” eravam = imperfect of essere “we were,” leggeri = “light / nimble.” The phrase says both were already light in step.
segments.purgatorio-12-g15: Ed el mi disse: ed = e + vowel elision (“and”), el = subject pronoun “he,” mi = dative/accusative clitic “to me,” disse = past of dire “said.” It introduces direct speech addressed to the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-12-g16: Volgi li occhi in giùe: Volgi (imperative of volgere) = “turn (direct)”; li occhi = “the eyes” (li = definite article); in giùe = archaic/poetic locution “downward.” The imperative addresses the narrator directly.
segments.purgatorio-12-g17: Buon ti sarà = “It will be good for you” (buon used predicatively; ti = dative “to you”; sarà = future of essere). The future gives advice about the effect of the action.
segments.purgatorio-12-g18: Per tranquillar la via: per + infinitive gives purpose “in order to calm / steady the way.” Tranquillar (infinitive, variant of tranquillare) = “to calm/steady”; la via = “the way / path.”
segments.purgatorio-12-g19: Veder lo letto de le piante tue: Veder (infinitive) “to see / looking at”; lo letto = “the bed” (here: ground/ground surface); de le = older form of di + le = “of the”; piante tue = “your soles / the soles of your feet” (piante = the undersides of the feet). The infinitive phrases state what will help steady the way.
segments.purgatorio-12-g20: "Come," here functions as an introductory particle meaning "as/inasmuch as/so" and links forward to the explanatory/purpose clause that follows. It is not a question word but a connective (poetic/archaic usage).
segments.purgatorio-12-g21: This is a purpose/reason clause: "perché ... sia" = "so that ... may be". "perché" has a purposive sense "so that"; "di lor memoria" = "of their memory/that their memory" ("lor" = their, archaic enclitic); "sia" is present subjunctive of essere (3rd sg.) used to express purpose/possibility: "may be/exist."
segments.purgatorio-12-g22: Word order: a fronted prepositional phrase "sovra i sepolti" ("above the buried") precedes the subject "le tombe terragne" ("the earthen tombs"). "Portan" is the archaic/dialectal present 3rd-person plural (modern "portano"); it governs the object clause. "Segnato" is a past participle used as part of the object phrase ("bear marked"); "quel ch'elli eran pria" = "that which they were before," with "ch'" = relative "che" and "elli" = archaic "they."
segments.purgatorio-12-g23: "Onde" here means "whereupon/therefore/so that," introducing the consequence. "Lì molte volte si ripiagne" is an impersonal/reflexive construction: "there many times one weeps again" ("ripiagne" = "weeps again" or laments anew). "Per la puntura de la rimembranza" gives the cause: "because of/through the sting of remembrance," where "de" = archaic "di."
segments.purgatorio-12-g24: Relative clause continuing from "puntura": "che solo a’ pïi dà de le calcagne" = "which gives of the heels only to the pious." "Solo" = "only;" "a’" is the contracted preposition "a + i/ai" = "to the;" "pïi" (pis with diaeresis) = "the pious." The idiom "dare de le calcagne" means to give a kick to the heels (figurative).
segments.purgatorio-12-g25: "sì vid' io lì," = "thus I saw there." "Sì" = "thus/so;" "vid'" is the poetical contracted form of the perfect/past "vidi" (I saw) with the final vowel dropped and an apostrophe; the subject pronoun "io" is emphatic and optional; "lì" = "there."
segments.purgatorio-12-g26: "ma di miglior sembianza secondo l’artificio," = "but with a better appearance according to (or by) artifice/craft." "Di miglior sembianza" is a descriptive phrase "of better appearance" ("di" = with/regarding); "secondo" = preposition "according to"; "l’artificio" = "the artifice/skill/craft," here meaning artistic crafting or arrangement.
segments.purgatorio-12-g27: This closes the thought: "figurato quanto per via di fuor del monte avanza." "Figurato" is a past participle ("represented/figured/portrayed") functioning as a predicate complement to what the speaker saw. "Quanto" = "how/ how far/ how much" and introduces the clause measured by the verb "avanza" (3rd sg. present "advances/extends"). "Per via di fuor del monte" = "by/along the way outside the mountain" ("fuor" = archaic "fuori").
segments.purgatorio-12-g28: 'Vedea' is the imperfect indicative of vedere (literally 'I saw' or 'I used to see'), with the subject (io) omitted; it introduces a past continuous/visual scene. 'colui' is the demonstrative pronoun 'that one / he' and is the object of vedea; the relative clause 'che fu nobil creato' modifies colui. 'fu nobil creato' is a passive perfect built with passato remoto 'fu' + past participle 'creato' (so 'was nobly created'); 'più ch’altra creatura' is a comparative phrase 'more than any other creature' with 'ch’' = 'che' used as 'than'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g29: 'giù dal cielo' is an adverbial phrase placed frontally meaning 'down from the sky'. 'folgoreggiando' is a gerund of folgoreggiare expressing manner ('flashing/dazzling like lightning') and modifies the following infinitive. 'scender' is the archaic infinitive (without final -e sometimes omitted in verse) meaning 'to descend'; here it belongs to an accusative–infinitive construction tied to 'vedea' (i.e. 'I saw him descend').
segments.purgatorio-12-g30: 'da l’un lato' = 'from one side / on one side'; 'da' + definite article 'l’' is a common Dantean contraction, and 'l’un' = 'the one' before 'lato' ('side'). The whole phrase is adverbial of direction/place modifying 'scender'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g31: 'Vedëa' (variant spelling with diaeresis) is again the imperfect 'I saw'. 'Brïareo' is the proper name Briareus. 'fitto dal telo celestïal giacer' groups an adjective/participle construction + agent/means + infinitive: 'fitto' = 'fixed/fastened', 'dal telo celestïal' = 'by/from the heavenly veil/cloth', and 'giacer' (infinitive) = 'to lie' functioning as the complement seen by the narrator ('I saw Briareus lying, fixed by the heavenly veil'). The order (adjective + agent + infinitive) is a poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-12-g32: 'da l’altra parte' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on the other side'; the article contraction 'l’' again precedes 'altra'. It signals a contrastive spatial position (what he saw on the opposite side).
segments.purgatorio-12-g33: 'grave a la terra' means 'heavy toward/upon the earth'—'grave' is a predicative adjective indicating that he was weighed down. 'per lo mortal gelo' gives the cause: 'because of/through the mortal (deadly) frost' ('per' marks cause and 'mortal gelo' intensifies the severity of the cold). Word order is slightly elevated/poetic but straightforward in meaning.
segments.purgatorio-12-g34: The repeated 'Vedea' marks successive sights. Here 'Vedea Timbreo,' names the first figure seen; the verb is the imperfect and 'Timbreo' functions as a proper noun (Dante's catalogue of figures).
segments.purgatorio-12-g35: Another 'vedea' continues the catalogue. 'Pallade e Marte' are the coordinated nominative names (Pallas/Athena and Mars) and function as additional objects of the narrative vision.
segments.purgatorio-12-g36: 'armati' is the plural past participle/adjective 'armed', agreeing with the plural subjects (Pallas and Mars); 'ancora' is the adverb 'still' indicating they remain armed. The participle is used predicatively after the nouns.
segments.purgatorio-12-g37: 'intorno al padre loro' is a prepositional phrase 'around their father'; 'intorno' = 'around', 'al' = 'to the/at the', and 'loro' is the postnominal possessive 'their' (common in older/poetic Italian to place the possessive after the noun).
segments.purgatorio-12-g38: 'mirar' is the infinitive 'to look at / to gaze upon' used as an infinitive complement in the visual catalogue (parallel to the earlier 'vedea ... scender'). 'le membra d’i Giganti sparte' is the direct object: 'the limbs of the Giants scattered' — 'd’i' = 'of the', 'Giganti' = 'Giants', and 'sparte' is a past participle modifying 'membra' meaning 'scattered' (placed after the noun for poetic effect).
segments.purgatorio-12-g39: 'Vedea Nembròt' again uses the imperfect 'I saw Nimrod'. 'a piè del gran lavoro' = 'at the foot of the great work' ('a piè' = 'at the foot/base of'); 'quasi smarrito' is a predicative phrase 'almost bewildered/lost' qualifying Nimrod. The sequence is a compact descriptive clause in Dantean style.
segments.purgatorio-12-g40: 'e riguardar' continues the narration with another infinitive complement 'and to regard/look upon'. 'le genti' is the object ('the peoples'). The relative clause 'che ’n Sennaàr con lui superbi fuoro' modifies 'genti': 'that in Shinar with him had been proud' — '’n' = 'in', 'Sennaàr' = Shinar, 'con lui' = 'with him', and 'fuoro' is passato remoto 'were'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g54: Mostrava is the imperfect (3rd singular) of mostrare meaning 'showed' or 'was showing' with an implicit subject (the pictured scene). Ancor is an adverb 'still'. Lo duro pavimento is the direct object: the masculine article lo precedes the adjective + noun ('the hard floor'). Come introduces the example clause: Almeon (proper name) a sua madre is the indirect object 'to his mother'. Fé is the archaic passato remoto (faire → fece) 'made/caused', followed by the infinitive parer 'to seem/appear' and the predicative adjective caro 'dear' — together 'made ... appear dear'. Lo sventurato addornamento is the infinitive's object with the article + adjective + noun ('the unfortunate decoration').
segments.purgatorio-12-g55: Mostrava come introduces the subordinate clause 'how...'. I figli is the subject 'the sons/children'. Si gittaro is an archaic passato remoto (gittarono) with the reflexive si: 'they threw themselves' or 'flung themselves'. Sovra is the preposition 'upon/on' and dentro dal tempio (dentro + dal) places the action 'inside the temple'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g56: E come repeats the introducer 'and how', linking a second example or episode to the first; come again introduces a new subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-12-g57: Morto is the past participle 'dead' used adjectivally; lui is the pronoun 'him'/'he'. The phrase morto lui is a participial clause meaning 'with him dead' or 'when he was dead', a compact temporal/absolute expression.
segments.purgatorio-12-g58: Quivi is an adverb 'there'. Il here functions as the masculine direct object pronoun 'him' (old usage where the article form serves pronominally). Lasciaro is the passato remoto (they left) of lasciare: 'they left him'. Word order is typical older Italian, with object pronoun preceding the verb as a clitic form.
segments.purgatorio-12-g59: Mostrava is 'it showed'. La ruina e ’l crudo scempio is the compound object: la (the) + ruina (ruin) and l' (the) + crudo scempio (the cruel slaughter/devastation). Che introduces the relative clause 'that Tamiri made/wrought'; fé is the archaic passato remoto of fare ('made/caused'), and Tamiri is the proper name performing the action.
segments.purgatorio-12-g60: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Disse is the passato remoto of dire 'said'. A Ciro is the indirect object 'to Cyrus.' The colon that follows introduces the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-12-g61: The speech begins with an exclamatory object 'Sangue' ('blood') followed by sitisti, an archaic form meaning 'you thirst' (2nd person present/preterite sense in older usage from 'sitire'/'sitare'). Grammatically the verb addresses an implied 'you' with 'blood' as the object or the thing craved.
segments.purgatorio-12-g62: E io di sangue t’empio is 'and I fill you with blood.' E = 'and'. Io = 'I' (subject explicit). Di + sangue marks the material/contents 'with blood'. T’ is the clitic for 'thee/you'. Empio here is the verb form 'I fill' (1st person singular present of empiere) — an older conjugation; though the lemma list gives adjectival senses, in context it functions as a verb.
segments.purgatorio-12-g63: Mostrava come again introduces 'how'. In rotta is an idiom meaning 'in rout' or 'in flight' (rotta here = 'rout'). Si fuggiro is an archaic passato remoto plural (fuggirono) with the reflexive si: 'they fled/put themselves to flight'. Li Assiri is the subject 'the Assyrians' ('li' old plural article).
segments.purgatorio-12-g64: Poi che introduces a temporal clause 'after/when', fu morto is a passive perfect (fu + past participle) 'was killed/was dead', and Oloferne is the proper name 'Holofernes'. The phrase gives the temporal circumstance for the rout.
segments.purgatorio-12-g65: E anche = 'and also/even'. Le reliquie is the plural noun 'the relics/remains'. Del is the contraction of di + il 'of the'. Martiro here refers to the martyr/martyrdom related to those relics; grammatically it's a genitive noun phrase 'of the martyr/martyrdom.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g114: An inverted, poetic clause: the noun voci (voices) is the subject, followed by the finite verb cantaron (past tense, passato remoto/archaic form) meaning 'sang'. The adverb sì follows the verb and modifies how they sang ('so' / 'so much' / 'so beautifully'). The initial apostrophe marks a poetic elision of a preceding sound and does not change the syntax here.
segments.purgatorio-12-g115: Che introduces a result/explanatory clause ('that'). Nol is the contracted non + lo (negation + accusative clitic) meaning 'it not' (= 'it could not'). Diria is an archaic/poetic conditional (3rd singular) of dire, 'would say' / 'could say'. Sermone (speech/discourse) is the grammatical subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion: literally 'speech would not say it', i.e. 'no speech could express it.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g116: This is an exclamative clause. Ahi is an interjection ('ah/alas'). Quanto works as an exclamative adverb ('how'). Son is the contracted present 'sono' (they/are). Diverse is the adjective agreeing in number/gender with quelle foci ('different'). Quelle foci is the demonstrative + noun 'those mouths' (foce = mouth/outlet). Da + l’ + infernali marks the comparison 'from the infernal (ones)', with the article and adjective infernali understood substantively ('the infernal [mouths]').
segments.purgatorio-12-g117: 'Ché' is a conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' introducing a causal explanation. 'Quivi' = 'there' (locative). 'Per canti' = 'by/through songs' (per + plural noun indicating the means or accompaniment). 'S’ entra' is the impersonal/reflexive construction: the clitic 's’' makes the verb 'entra' impersonal ('one enters' / 'people enter' / 'is entered into'), so the clause reads 'for there one enters through songs.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g118: A coordinated clause: 'e' = 'and'; 'là giù' = 'down there' (là = 'there', giù = 'down'); 'per lamenti feroci' = 'by/through fierce laments', where 'per' again marks means/instrument and 'feroci' modifies 'lamenti'. It mirrors the first clause but places the action lower down.
segments.purgatorio-12-g119: Già is an adverb placed before the verb meaning 'already' or 'by then.' Montavam is the imperfect indicative of montare in a poetic/Tuscan form (‑avam) and here functions as 'we were climbing' (progressive past). Su per li scaglion santi is a prepositional phrase 'up along the holy steps': per + noun indicates movement 'along/through/by' the steps, li is the plural article, scaglion is an archaic/plural form (modern scaglioni) meaning 'large steps/stairs,' and santi is the plural adjective 'holy/sacred' agreeing with scaglion.
segments.purgatorio-12-g120: Ed links this clause to the previous one ('and'). Esser is the infinitive 'to be' used as the grammatical subject (an infinitival noun) of the impersonal verb parea. Mi is the dative 'to me' with parea, and parea is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of parere 'to seem' — so 'it seemed to me.' Troppo più lieve is the degree phrase 'much/far lighter/easier' (troppo + più intensifies lieve). Che introduces the comparative clause 'than...' (here 'than walking on the plain had seemed to me before'). In the comparative clause, per lo pian means 'on/over the plain' (per = along/through, lo = the), non negates the verb, mi again marks 'to me,' parea repeats 'seemed,' and davanti (here temporal) means 'before/previously.' Note the overall structure: 'and to be there seemed to me much easier than (it) did on the plain before.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g41: A simple vocative address: 'O' is the interjection calling out to the addressee; 'Nïobè' is the proper name Niobe (diaeresis marks the separate vowel).
segments.purgatorio-12-g42: 'con che occhi dolenti' = 'with what sorrowful eyes' (con + interrogative/ exclamative che = 'with what'); 'vedea' is the imperfect tense (I saw/was seeing) used narratively; word order ved ea io te places the verb before the pronoun for emphasis. 'te segnata' uses the past participle segnata as a predicate/adjectival complement meaning 'marked' (you having been marked), and 'in su la strada' is a locative phrase 'upon the road' (in su = upon).
segments.purgatorio-12-g43: 'tra sette e sette' is a poetic way of saying 'seven and seven' (i.e. fourteen) placed before the noun; 'tuoi figliuoli' is the possessive + noun 'your sons'; 'spenti' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'extinguished' / 'dead'. The whole is an exclamatory remark on the number of slain children.
segments.purgatorio-12-g44: Vocative address again: 'O' is the interjection; 'Saùl' names the biblical Saul (the reference is to his death).
segments.purgatorio-12-g45: 'come' introduces an exclamatory 'how'; 'in su la propria spada' = 'upon your own sword' (in su = upon, propria agrees with spada); 'quivi' = 'there'; 'parevi' is the imperfect 'you seemed/appeared' (2nd sing.), and 'morto' is a predicate participle/adjective 'dead'; 'in Gelboè' gives the place 'at Gilboa'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g46: 'che' here introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/and that'); 'poi' = 'afterward'; 'non sentì' = 'did not feel/experience' (sentì used narratively = 'experienced'); 'pioggia né rugiada' = 'neither rain nor dew' with 'né' coordinating the negatives.
segments.purgatorio-12-g47: Vocative again: 'O' interjection + 'folle' an adjective ('foolish, mad') placed before the noun for emphasis; 'Aragne' is 'Arachne' / 'spider' (mythical weaver).
segments.purgatorio-12-g48: 'sì' = 'so/thus'; 'vedea' is the imperfect 'I saw'; inverted order 'vedea io te' places emphasis on the seeing; 'già' = 'already'; 'mezza ragna' = 'half spider' (mezza as 'half' modifying ragna).
segments.purgatorio-12-g49: 'trista' = 'sad/wretched'; 'in su li stracci de l’opera' = 'upon the tatters/rags of the work' (in su = upon; li = archaic plural article); the relative clause 'che mal per te si fé' means 'which was badly made on/in consequence of you' — 'si fé' is an impersonal/passive archaic past form of fare ('was made').
segments.purgatorio-12-g51: 'già' = 'already'; 'non par' = 'it does not seem' (parere, impersonally 'it seems not'); 'che' introduces the subordinate clause; 'minacci' is a subjunctive/present form meaning 'threaten' (here 'threatens'); 'quivi' = 'there'; '’l tuo segno' = 'your standard/sign' (subject of 'minacci').
segments.purgatorio-12-g52: 'ma' = 'but'; 'pien di spavento' = 'full of terror' (pien = filled with, spavento = fright/terror). The phrase 'nel porta un carro' has 'nel' = 'in the/into the' and 'porta' here functions as 'bears/carries' (third-person present of portare) referring back to the sign/standard; 'un carro' = 'a chariot/wagon'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g53: 'sanza' = 'without'; 'ch’ altri il cacci' = 'that another drive it away' — 'altri' = 'another/others'; 'il' = 'it' (referring to the cart or sign); 'cacci' is the present subjunctive/optative form of cacciare used here to mean 'drive out/expel'. The whole phrase means 'without anyone driving it away.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g66: Vedeva is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of vedere and gives a continuous/past narrative sense 'he saw' or 'he was seeing'. Troia is the direct object (proper noun). The two prepositional phrases in cenere e in caverne locate the scene ('in ashes and in caverns'); caverne is a poetic plural of caverna.
segments.purgatorio-12-g67: o here is an interjection of address ('O', not the disjunctive 'or') introducing a vocative; Ilïón (Ilium) is being apostrophized — a poetic direct address to Troy.
segments.purgatorio-12-g68: come introduces an exclamation meaning 'how'; te is the accusative of the vocative 'you' (Troy personified) and is the object described by the adjectives basso e vile ('low and base'). Mostrava is imperfect 'showed' and il segno is the subject/agent phrase 'the sign' which mostrava. The relative clause che lì si discerne uses impersonal/reflexive si + present discerne to mean 'that is/was discerned there' (i.e. 'the mark that can be seen there'). Note the poetic inversion: the adjectives follow te and modify it.
segments.purgatorio-12-g69: This whole line poses a rhetorical question. Qual di pennel fu maestro o di stile = 'what master of the brush or of style was there' (fu = simple past 'was'). The relative clause che ritraesse uses the imperfect subjunctive ritraesse ('who could/would portray') to express hypothetical or potential skill in the past. The phrase l'ombre e ’ tratti = 'the shades and the strokes/features' (objects of ritraesse). The sequence ch'ivi mirar farieno is a poetic inversion: mirar (infinitive 'to be looked at') follows farieno ('would make' — an archaic conditional form), so together they mean 'which a subtle genius would make to be seen there' (uno ingegno sottile is the agent/idea of refined talent); in short, 'which even a subtle genius would cause to be seen.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g70: This is a balanced, almost proverb-like statement. Morti li morti uses an emphatic fronted adjective/noun order ('dead the dead') and then e i vivi parean vivi = 'and the living appeared living.' Parean is the imperfect indicative (3rd pl.) of parere 'to seem/appear'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g71: non vide mei di me chi vide il vero is a comparative construction: non ... chi ... = 'no one ... who ...'; mei di me is an archaic/comparative phrasing ('more of me' / 'more truly of me'); vide (past) is 'saw'. The line means roughly 'no one saw me more truly than he who saw the truth.' Chi is the relative/indefinite 'who' and il vero = 'the truth.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g72: quant' io calcai is a temporal/comparative phrase: quant' is short for quanto 'as much/as far as'; io is the subject 'I'; calcai is passato remoto (1st sing.) of calcare 'to tread/step on' meaning 'as I went on / as I trod.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g73: fin che = 'until'. Chinato is a past participle/adjective 'bowed' or 'bent over' describing the subject; givi is an archaic 1st-person past form of givo (here 'to be useful / to help'), so chinato givi = 'until, bent over, I was of use' or 'until I stooped and was useful.' The line uses an older verb form and a participial clause with the subject omitted (still 'I').
segments.purgatorio-12-g74: 'Or' is an adverb meaning 'now' (vocative or hortatory opening). 'Superbite' is an imperative (2nd person plural) formed from the noun/virtue 'superbita' used here as a verbalized command: 'be proud' or 'take pride'. The phrase functions as an exhortation to the addressed group.
segments.purgatorio-12-g75: The short clause 'e via' functions like an instruction 'and away/and on' (via here = 'away/along/onward'); 'col' is the contracted form of 'con il' ('with the'); 'viso' = 'face'; 'altero' is an adjective meaning 'haughty, proud'. The whole string describes how they should go: 'and on with a haughty face.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g76: 'Figliuoli d’Eva' is a vocative address: 'children/sons of Eve' (a way of addressing humanity). 'd’' is the contracted preposition 'di' = 'of'. The phrase names the addressees directly.
segments.purgatorio-12-g77: This is a negative imperative instruction. 'E non chinate' = 'and do not bend/lower' (imperative plural with 'non'); 'il volto' = 'the face'. 'Sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'so as to'); 'veggiate' is the 2nd-person plural present (here functioning as a result clause verb — historically treated like a subjunctive/optative) meaning 'you may see' or 'you come to see'; 'il vostro mal sentero' = 'your evil path' (mal + sentero, older spelling of sentiero). Together: 'and do not lower your face so that you may see your evil path.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g89: Io era ben del suo ammonir uso pur di non perder tempo: 'Io' is the subject 'I'. 'era' is the imperfect of essere ('was') establishing a past habitual state. 'del' = di + il links 'suo ammonir' to 'uso' (literally 'of the his-admonish use'); 'ammonir' is an infinitive used substantively ('his admonishing'), and 'uso' means 'habit/being accustomed' (adjectival/noun). 'pur di' introduces purpose or restriction with the following infinitive 'non perder tempo' ('in order not to waste time').
segments.purgatorio-12-g90: sì che ’n quella materia non potea parlarmi chiuso: 'sì che' introduces a result/clause ('so that'). The contracted form '’n' (= 'in') precedes 'quella materia' ('that subject/matter'). 'potea' is the imperfect of potere ('could/was able to') and governs the infinitive 'parlarmi' ('speak to me'). 'chiuso' is an adjective modifying the manner of speaking — figuratively 'reserved' or 'closed' — so the whole clause says he was not able to speak to me in a reserved way.
segments.purgatorio-12-g91: A noi venìa la creatura bella: the prepositional phrase 'A noi' ('to us/toward us') is fronted for emphasis; 'venìa' is the imperfect 'was coming' (ongoing/past action); 'la creatura bella' is the subject ('the beautiful creature'). The order puts motion toward the speaker first.
segments.purgatorio-12-g92: biancovestito e ne la faccia quale par tremolando mattutina stella: 'biancovestito' is an adjective 'white-robed'. 'ne' here is an adverbial/poetic form meaning 'in' or 'on' (as in 'in/on her face') and is followed by the article 'la' and noun 'faccia' ('face'). 'quale' introduces a simile ('which/as'), 'par' is the present 'seems' used in this descriptive clause, and 'tremolando' is a present participle describing the star's quivering; 'mattutina stella' = 'morning star'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g93: Le braccia aperse,: an elliptical nominative/accusative phrase meaning 'with her arms opened' or 'her arms open'. 'Le' is the feminine plural article; 'aperse' is a past participle used adjectivally and agrees in number/gender with 'braccia'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g94: e indi aperse l’ale;: 'e' = 'and'. 'indi' is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon' (poetic/archaic) introducing the next action. 'aperse' is the past tense form 'opened/spread' (agreement with the feminine subject), and 'l’ale' = 'the wings' (ale, plural).
segments.purgatorio-12-g95: disse:: 'disse' is the passato remoto (narrative past) of dire, 'said', introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-12-g96: «Venite:: 'Venite' is the second-person plural imperative (or present indicative used as exhortation) meaning 'Come!' addressed to those present.
segments.purgatorio-12-g97: qui son presso i gradi,: 'qui' = 'here'; 'son' is the contracted present of essere ('sono' -> 'are'); 'presso' = 'near' or 'close to'; 'i gradi' = 'the steps' or 'degrees' (here physical steps). The clause states location: the steps are near here.
segments.purgatorio-12-g98: e agevolemente omai si sale.: 'e' = 'and'. 'agevolemente' (agevolmente) = 'easily/readily'. 'omai' = 'by now/now already'. 'si sale' is an impersonal or reflexive present construction meaning 'one goes up' or 'it is easy to go up' (literally 'one climbs'). 'sale' is the present of salire.
segments.purgatorio-12-g99: A questo invito vegnon molto radi:: 'A questo invito' = 'to this invitation' (indicating response to what was said). 'vegnon' is a poetic/dialectal form of vennero/venivano ('came'); 'molto radi' = 'very sparsely' or 'very few' (i.e. few came). The phrase means that few responded to the invitation.
segments.purgatorio-12-g100: o gente umana,: a vocative address. 'O' is the exclamatory vocative 'O/Oh', 'gente umana' = 'human people' or 'humanity'—a direct address to people in general.
segments.purgatorio-12-g101: per volar sù nata,: 'per' here introduces purpose/characteristic ('born to/for'); 'volar sù' = 'to fly up' (infinitive + adverb 'up'); 'nata' is a past participle/adjective 'born' agreeing with the feminine noun it refers to (gente) — 'born to fly upward'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g102: perché a poco vento così cadi?»: 'perché' here is interrogative 'why'. 'a poco vento' idiomatically means 'at the slightest wind/with little wind' (i.e. at a small breeze); 'così' = 'so/thus'. 'cadi' is the present indicative 'you fall' (addressing people generally), giving the rhetorical question 'why do you fall so from so slight a wind?'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g78: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted imperfect statement. Più era già (lit. "more was already") functions as a temporal/intensifying phrase meaning "by then" or "already further." Per noi marks the relation "for us," and del monte vòlto uses vòlto (past participle of volgere) adjectivally to mean "turned away from the mountain." Del cammin del sole = "of the sun’s course/path," with assai più speso (speso used adverbially, "much more often/for much more of it"). The comparative che … non stimava links to the following clause: che ("than") introduces what the speaker’s anxious soul did not reckon; non stimava is imperfect "did not suppose/reckon," and animo non sciolto is a fixed phrase "mind/spirit not yet loosened/at ease."
segments.purgatorio-12-g79: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause: "when." Colui che … andava is a relative clause with colui ("that one/he who") as head. Sempre innanzi atteso is a poetic phrase: innanzi atteso (atteso used as a past participle/adjectival idea) = "ever/always ahead" or "ever before us." Andava is the imperfect of andare ("was going/proceeding"), expressing an action ongoing at the same time as the main clause.
segments.purgatorio-12-g80: Cominciò is passato remoto (third singular) of cominciare: a simple past narrative verb meaning "he began" and here introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-12-g81: Drizza is the imperative (2nd person singular) of drizzare meaning "straighten/raise/direct," here an order: "Raise/straighten the head." La testa is the direct object; word order is normal for an imperative + object.
segments.purgatorio-12-g82: Non è più tempo di introduces a fixed idiom: "it is no longer time to …" Non è (negative + present of essere) = "it is not"; più here has the temporal value "any longer." Di + infinitive (di gir) marks purpose/what it is no longer time for: gir = arcane infinitive of girare/andare "to go/on"; sì sospeso is an adverb + adjective phrase "so suspended/hesitating," with sospeso used figuratively for "in suspense/hesitating."
segments.purgatorio-12-g83: Vedi is the imperative/indicative "see/behold." Colà is an adverb "there." Un angel che s'appresta uses the reflexive pronoun s' (si) with appresta (present tense) to mean "prepares himself/gets ready," and per venir verso noi is an infinitival purpose phrase "to come toward us" (per + venire = "in order to come/coming").
segments.purgatorio-12-g84: Vedi che torna is again "see that (it) returns/notice that (she) returns;" torna is present tense of tornare "returns." Dal servigio del dì = "from the service of the day" (dal = da + il). L'ancella sesta = "the sixth handmaid/maid of the sixth hour" (sesta here indicates the sixth hour/noon; ancella = handmaiden).
segments.purgatorio-12-g85: Imperative addorna (2nd person singular) "adorn/arrange" governs two objects: il viso (the face) and li atti (the gestures). Di reverenza ("with reverence") indicates the manner or quality with which the face and gestures should be adorned.
segments.purgatorio-12-g86: Sì che introduces a result clause "so that/so as to." I diletti is the plural noun "the delights" (here: things that please), lo 'nvïarci is a clitic-infinitive construction meaning "[may] send us" (lo is pleonastic/neutral here, nvïarci = inviare + ci "send us"), and in suso means "upwards/up there."
segments.purgatorio-12-g87: Pensa (imperative or present used as command) "think/consider" introduces a subordinate clause: che questo dì mai non raggiorna. Questo dì = "this day;" mai non raggiorna (mai with non gives "never") and raggiorna is present (or present-subjunctive/indicative depending on reading) of raggiornare used here to mean "returns/recurs" — thus: "think that this day never returns."
segments.purgatorio-12-g88: This segment contains only a period (full stop), i.e. punctuation, not lexical content. Grammatically it marks the end of the preceding sentence and carries no words to analyze; there are no verb forms, inflections, or poetic contractions here.
segments.purgatorio-12-g103: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a single period) and carries no lexical items to analyze; it functions as a pause or line break in the poem.
segments.purgatorio-12-g104: Menocci is a proper name or local name placed before a relative clause; ove introduces that clause meaning 'where'. Era + past participle tagliata describes a past state (imperfect tense indicating a continuing or backgrounded past condition): 'the rock was cut/ had been cut.' The whole phrase names a place: 'Menocci, where the rock had been cut.'
segments.purgatorio-12-g105: Quivi = 'there' (locative adverb). Mi is the first‑person object pronoun ('to/against me'). Batté is the third‑person singular passato remoto of battere (narrative past: 'struck' or 'beat'); the subject is omitted and understood from context. L'ali is the article + ali (poetic form: le ali → l'ali) meaning 'the wings'; per la fronte = 'upon/against the brow' (per here indicates contact/location).
segments.purgatorio-12-g106: Poi = 'then/afterwards'. Mi = indirect object 'to me'. Promise is the third‑person passato remoto of promettere (older form spelled promise), meaning 'he/it promised/assured'. Sicura is an adjective ('safe/secure') that functions as a predicative descriptor tied to l'andata (the following noun); the word order (adj. before noun) is stylistic but the adjective still qualifies l'andata ('the going/passage'). L'andata = 'the passage/the way up' (lit. 'the going').
segments.purgatorio-12-g107: Come = 'as/just as' introducing a simile. A man destra is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on the right hand/side'; man is a poetic/archaic form for mano ('hand') and destra is the adjective 'right' (here 'right hand'). Word order (a + noun + adjective) is idiomatic and poetic.
segments.purgatorio-12-g108: Per + infinitive (per salire) expresses purpose ('in order to climb'). Al is the contraction of a + il ('to the'). Dove siede uses the present indicative siede ('sits / is located') to describe a place. The relative clause che soggioga la ben guidata sopra Rubaconte modifies chiesa: che = 'which/that' and soggioga is present tense meaning 'subjugates/controls' (here governing whatever 'la ben guidata' refers to). Sopra Rubaconte is a locative phrase 'above Rubaconte' (Rubaconte is used as a proper name/reference).
segments.purgatorio-12-g109: Si rompe is an impersonal/reflexive construction (here 'is broken' or 'breaks') describing the effect on l'ardita foga ('the bold passion/ardor') caused by something. Del montar = 'of the mounting/the ascent' (del = di + il). Per le scalee = 'by/through the stairways' (per as causal/instrumental). Che si fero ad etade is a relative clause: che = 'which/that'; si + an archaic past form fero is intended as a passive/reflexive past ('were made' in older usage) and ad etade = 'in/at (former) age' (i.e. long ago). Ch'era sicuro il quaderno e la doga: ch'era (imperfect) describes the resulting state ('so that the quaderno and the doga were secure'); the imperfect here pictures a background or ongoing condition.
segments.purgatorio-12-g110: Così = 'thus/so' linking the simile's result. S'allenta is reflexive present indicative ('loosens itself' / 'gives way'). La ripa che cade = 'the bank/cliff that falls/crumbles' (che introduces a relative clause describing the bank). Quivi = 'there'; ben ratta is an adverbial phrase where ben intensifies ratta (here taken as 'swift/quick' or 'readily'), so 'quite quickly'; da l'altro girone = 'from the other ring/turn' (da indicates separation or origin).
segments.purgatorio-12-g111: Ma = 'but'. Quinci e quindi = 'from here and from there' (quinci 'on this side/from here', quindi 'on that side/from there'), a paired locution. L'alta pietra = 'the high/tall stone' (article + adjective + noun). Rade is the adjective rado in plural form agreeing with pietra in Dante's metrical usage; here it means 'sparse/ worn/thinned' — describing how the high stone is worn away or thin on both sides.
segments.purgatorio-12-g112: Noi = 'we'. Volgendo is the present gerund (or present participial form used absolutely) of volgere ('turning'), used here to indicate an action contemporaneous with what follows: 'we, turning...'. Ivi = 'there'. Le nostre persone = 'our persons/bodies/faces' (persone in older Italian often means physical persons or bodies). The construction is an absolute/participial clause providing background action.
segments.purgatorio-12-g113: This is a direct quotation in Latin: 'Beati pauperes spiritu' = 'Blessed are the poor in spirit.' Grammatically, it is the Latin Beatitude (Beati + nominative plural pauperes; spiritu is the ablative of manner/description). Dante reproduces the liturgical/Latin formula directly, so the words are not in vernacular Italian here but are cited as spoken text.
segments.purgatorio-12-g121: Ond' is a contracted poetic form of 'onde' meaning 'thereupon' or 'so', placed before the subject io to introduce the speaker's speech; the colon marks that what follows is direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-12-g122: Maestro is a vocative addressing Dante's guide (Virgil); no verb is present here because the imperative that follows governs the request.
segments.purgatorio-12-g123: Dì is the singular imperative of dire ('say/tell'), used as a direct command/request to the addressee; the object ('to me' or the content) is supplied by the following clause.
segments.purgatorio-12-g124: Qual cosa greve levata s’è da me is a nominal clause meaning 'what heavy thing has been lifted from me'. Levata is the past participle placed before the auxiliary (poetic inversion), s’ is the reflexive/clitic particle (si) attached to the auxiliary è, and da me specifies the source ('from me').
segments.purgatorio-12-g125: Che introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that...', nulla quasi means 'almost nothing' (nulla modified by quasi), and per me fatica is a noun phrase 'for me a toil' (i.e. 'as a toil to me'), with per marking the experiencer/beneficiary.
segments.purgatorio-12-g126: Andando is the gerund of andare ('going') used adverbially to indicate the circumstance ('as I go' / 'while walking').
segments.purgatorio-12-g127: Si riceve is a passive/impersonal construction with the clitic si plus present riceve ('is received/experienced'); here it means 'is felt/experienced' — the clitic si makes the verb impersonal or reflexive.
segments.purgatorio-12-g128: A single period marking the end of the previous line of verse; no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-12-g129: Rispuose is the archaic past tense (passato remoto) of rispondere, meaning 'he answered'; the colon introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-12-g130: Questo is a temporal (when) clause: Quando introduces 'when...'. The sequence i P che son rimasi ancor nel volto tuo is a noun phrase with a relative clause: che son rimasi ancor = 'which have remained still' (son rimasi = sono rimasti, an archaic form). Presso che stinti is an idiom meaning 'almost faded' (presso che = almost).
segments.purgatorio-12-g131: Saranno is the future tense (third-person plural) of essere, 'they will be', picking up the subject introduced by the preceding Quando clause.
segments.purgatorio-12-g132: Com' è l'un is a short comparative clause 'as the one (is)' where com' is the contracted form of come and l'un means 'the one'; the clause alludes to an earlier example ('like the one').
segments.purgatorio-12-g133: Del tutto is an adverbial phrase 'completely' and rasi is the past participle (plural) of rasare 'to shave/erase' meaning 'utterly shaved away'; word order places the adverbial phrase before the participle in a compact poetic expression.
segments.purgatorio-12-g134: This clause describes the consequence: li tuoi piè ('your feet') is the subject; dal buon voler ('by good will') is an agent/means phrase introduced by da + article; sì vinti = 'so conquered/overcome' (vinti = past participle 'defeated/overcome'). The first word (fier) in the text is irregular/uncertain in form and is treated here as a textual irregularity.
segments.purgatorio-12-g135: Che introduces the result clause: non pur non fatica sentiranno is a rhetorical double negation meaning 'they will not only not feel fatigue' — pur here intensifies or frames the 'not only' sense; sentiranno is future 'they will feel'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g136: Ma introduces the contrast 'but', fia is an archaic future/subjunctive form of essere ('it will be'), diletto loro ('a delight to them') is the predicate, and esser sù pinti is an infinitival phrase 'to be up painted/ornamented' with sù marking upward placement; pinti is a past participle ('painted/marked') and here denotes a pleasurable condition rather than a physical painting in some readings.
segments.purgatorio-12-g137: Allor 'then' + fec' io is the contracted past 'feci io' ('I did'), a common archaic/poetic past-tense narrative; come introduces a simile 'like those who...', and the relative clause that follows describes those people.
segments.purgatorio-12-g138: Se non che is an idiom meaning 'except that'; cenni (gestures) altrui (to others) sospecciar fanno = 'make others suspect' where fanno is present tense 'they make' and the object of sospettar is the implied action/gesture.
segments.purgatorio-12-g139: Per che introduces a causal/result clause 'therefore/for which reason'; la mano ad accertar s’aiuta is reflexive: the hand 'helps itself to ascertain' (s'aiuta = si aiuta) — ad + infinitive is 'to'/'in order to' plus the infinitive accertar ('to ascertain').
segments.purgatorio-12-g140: A series of coordinated verbs: e cerca e truova e quello officio adempie — 'and it seeks and finds and fulfils that duty', where truova is a present-habitual/presentive form (narrative present or subjunctive flavor) and adempie is present indicative 'fulfills'. The relative clause che non si può fornir per la veduta modifies 'quello officio', meaning 'which cannot be supplied by sight' (non si può fornir = it cannot be provided; per la veduta = by sight/visual means).
segments.purgatorio-12-g141: E con le dita de la destra introduces the means 'and with the fingers of the right hand'; the next word (scempie) is a textual/lexical irregularity here (in context it functions adverbially to signal 'by feeling/feeling about'), and trovai is the first-person past 'I found' (trovai pur sei le lettere = 'I still/indeed found six letters'). The clause that follows identifies the letters and the agent who incised them.
segments.purgatorio-12-g142: A che guardando = 'upon which, looking' — a che ('to/at which' or 'upon which') introduces the link to the following action, and guardando is a gerund 'while/when looking'.
segments.purgatorio-12-g143: Il mio duca sorrise is simple past narration: il mio duca ('my duke/guide') + sorrise (passato remoto of sorridere, 'smiled'). The whole clause reports Virgil's reaction to what Dante found.

sentences.purgatorio-12-s1: In like manner, as oxen go yoked together, I was going with that burdened soul, until the gentle pedagogue allowed it.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s2: But when he said, “Leave him and cross over; for here each man fares well with wings and with oars — however much he can, let each propel his own boat”; I straightened up as one must to go and resumed with my body, although my thoughts remained bowed and dull.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s3: I had moved, and gladly followed my master’s steps, and the two of us already showed how light we were; and he said to me: “Turn your eyes downward: it will be good for you, to steady the way, to see the ground beneath your feet.”
sentences.purgatorio-12-s4: Since (or so that) their memory may remain, over the buried the earthen tombs bear marked what they were before; whereupon many times there one weeps again from the sting of remembrance, which gives a kick to the heels only of the pious; thus I saw there, but fashioned with a better appearance by artifice, how far the outward way of the mountain advances.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s5: I saw him — he who had been nobly created more than any other creature — descend from the sky, flashing like lightning, from one side.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s6: I saw Briareus lying fastened by the heavenly veil; on the other side I saw him heavy to the earth because of the mortal frost.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s7: I saw Timbreo, I saw Pallas and Mars, still armed, around their father, gazing at the limbs of the Giants scattered.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s8: I saw Nimrod at the foot of the great work, almost bewildered, and I saw him regard the peoples who in Shinar with him had been proud.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s13: It still showed the hard pavement, how Almeon made the ill-fated ornament appear dear to his mother.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s14: It showed how the sons flung themselves upon Sennacherib inside the temple, and how, when he was dead, they left him there.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s15: It showed the ruin and the cruel slaughter that Tamiri wrought, when she said to Cyrus: 'You thirst for blood, and I fill you with blood.'
sentences.purgatorio-12-s16: It showed how the Assyrians fled in rout, after Holofernes was slain, and even the relics of the martyr.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s33: Voices sang so, that no speech could tell it.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s34: Ah, how different are those mouths from the infernal ones!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s35: For there one enters through songs, and down there through fierce laments.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s36: We were already climbing up the holy steps, and being there seemed to me much lighter than walking on the plain had seemed to me before.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s9: O Niobe, with what sorrowful eyes I beheld you marked upon the road, among your seven and seven sons extinguished!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s10: O Saul, how there on your own sword you appeared dead at Gilboa, and afterwards felt neither rain nor dew!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s11: O foolish Arachne, so I saw you already half a spider, sad upon the rags of the work that was ill made because of you.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s12: O Rehoboam, your standard already no longer seems to threaten there; but, full of terror, it bears a chariot into the gate, without anyone driving it away.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s17: He saw Troy in ashes and in caverns; O Ilion — how low and base the sign there made you appear!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s18: What master of brush or of style was there who could have depicted the shadows and the strokes which a subtle mind would have caused to be seen there?
sentences.purgatorio-12-s19: The dead appeared dead and the living appeared alive: no one saw me more truly than he who saw the truth, as I walked, until, bowed over, I was of use.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s20: Now be proud, and go on with a haughty face, children of Eve, and do not bend your face so that you may see your evil path!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s25: I was well accustomed to his admonishing, just so as not to lose time, so that in that matter he could not speak to me in a reserved way.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s26: Toward us came the beautiful creature, white-robed, and on her face, as a trembling morning star seems.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s27: Her arms opened, and then she spread her wings; she said: 'Come: here the steps are near, and now one goes up easily.'
sentences.purgatorio-12-s28: At this invitation very few came: 'O human people, born to fly upward, why do you fall so at a little wind?'
sentences.purgatorio-12-s21: By then we had already turned farther from the mountain and had spent much more of the sun’s course than my not-yet-loosened spirit had reckoned, when he who always went ahead began: "Raise your head; it is no longer time to go on so suspended."
sentences.purgatorio-12-s22: Look there—an angel preparing himself to come toward us; see how the sixth handmaid returns from the service of the day.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s23: Adorn your face and your gestures with reverence, so that delights may send us up there; think that this day never returns!
sentences.purgatorio-12-s30: Menocci, where the rock had been cut; there its wings struck my brow; then he assured me the passage was safe.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s31: Just as, on the right hand, in climbing the mountain where the church sits that subjugates the well‑guided above Rubaconte, the bold ardor of the ascent is broken by the stairways that were made in former times, which made the quaderno and the doga secure; so the bank that falls there loosens, quite quickly, from the other ring; but here and there the high stone is worn thin.
sentences.purgatorio-12-s32: We, turning our persons there, 'Blessed are the poor in spirit!'
sentences.purgatorio-12-s37: Whereupon I said, "Master, tell me, what heavy thing has been lifted from me, that I almost feel no toil as I walk?"
sentences.purgatorio-12-s39: He answered: "When the P that still remain upon your brow, almost faded, shall be, like the one, entirely shaved off, your feet will be so overcome by good will that they will not only not feel fatigue, but it will be their delight to be painted up."
sentences.purgatorio-12-s40: Then I did as those who go with something on their head unknown to them, except that their gestures make others suspect it; therefore the hand helps itself to ascertain, and it searches and finds and accomplishes that office which cannot be performed by sight; and with the fingers of my right hand — by feeling — I still found the six letters that he had cut with the keys above my temples: upon which, looking, my duke smiled.
